quick_facts.label: Role scope
quick_facts.value: “Senior Lab Technician” is an employer-specific senior role rather than a single nationally uniform job specification: government postings show differing requirements and duties across hospital, public-health, research and laboratory settings.

quick_facts.label: Professional context
quick_facts.value: In clinical settings, the role sits within Medical Laboratory Sciences: the National Commission for Allied and Healthcare Professions Act lists Medical Lab Technologist among medical laboratory professionals who perform clinical tests on bodily-fluid and tissue specimens.

quick_facts.label: Typical senior-entry pattern
quick_facts.value: Recruiters commonly seek a relevant laboratory qualification plus prior technician experience. Examples include a diploma/degree in laboratory technology with 5/3 years’ experience, and life-science qualifications with 5/3 years’ TB-bacteriology experience for an ICMR project post.

quick_facts.label: Indicative progression
quick_facts.value: A National Career Service career map places Senior Lab Technician after Junior Lab Technician and Lab Technician/Assistant, with Lab Manager as a subsequent step; this is indicative, not a universal promotion rule.

sections.heading: Responsibilities
paragraphs: A senior technician’s work can combine advanced bench testing with quality and operational responsibility. In an ICMR National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme project, the stated duties included culture, drug-susceptibility and molecular drug-resistance testing; preparing external-quality-assessment materials; maintaining culture/DNA banks; proficiency testing and retesting; monitoring laboratory performance; compiling data; and entering information in Nikshay/LIMS.

The same ICMR role explicitly included assisting assessment visits, facilitating laboratory training programmes, maintaining equipment and conducting periodic calibration. In a hospital diagnostic-centre project at Lady Hardinge Medical College, the role included receiving, labelling and analysing blood samples, phlebotomy, and screening/diagnostic genetic tests. Accordingly, senior technicians should expect the exact mix of testing, staff support, records and quality work to depend on the laboratory’s specialty and employer.
bullets: Perform and document specialised tests using the laboratory’s approved procedures.
Support quality-assurance activities, including proficiency testing, performance monitoring and retesting where applicable.
Maintain equipment and complete scheduled calibration or maintenance records.
Use laboratory information systems and programme reporting systems where required.
Coach or support junior staff and training activities when assigned.

sections.heading: Entry routes and eligibility
paragraphs: There is no single all-India eligibility rule attached to the title “Senior Lab Technician”; applicants should treat the relevant recruitment notice as controlling. A Paradip Port Authority medical-department selection criterion specified a diploma or degree in laboratory technology plus experience as a laboratory technician—five years for diploma holders and three years for degree holders. Its written-test syllabus covered anatomy and physiology, biochemistry, microbiology, pathology, haematology, blood banking, histopathology and related laboratory techniques.

Specialised public-health and research posts may set different academic routes. An ICMR TB-bacteriology project accepted either specified BSc life-science subjects, with or without DMLT, plus five years’ TB-bacteriology experience, or specified MSc subjects, with or without DMLT, plus three years’ experience. A Lady Hardinge Medical College project post instead required a bachelor’s degree in Medical Laboratory Technology and two years of laboratory experience. These examples show why candidates should match their qualification, specialty experience and any required test or skill assessment to the specific vacancy.
bullets: Build experience first in a Lab Technician or Medical Laboratory Technology role, particularly in the intended specialty.
Hold the precise diploma, degree or discipline named in the advertisement; employers may distinguish between MLT and life-science pathways.
Document hands-on experience, instruments, laboratory information systems, quality processes and specialty techniques relevant to the vacancy.
Check whether selection includes a written test, skill test, interview, registration requirement or employer-specific age/category conditions.

sections.heading: Skills and knowledge
paragraphs: The core skill set is practical laboratory competence at a level appropriate to the specialty: specimen handling, accurate testing, documentation and safe use of laboratory instruments. For clinical laboratory work, the NCAHP Act describes medical and pathology laboratory professionals as formally trained people who perform clinical tests on bodily fluids and tissues to obtain information about a patient’s health or cause of death.

For senior roles, employers also value specialty testing, quality assurance and data capability. The ICMR project description calls for culture and molecular testing, external quality assessment, proficiency testing, equipment calibration, laboratory-performance monitoring and Nikshay/LIMS data entry. The Paradip Port syllabus additionally illustrates the breadth often expected in medical-laboratory recruitment, including microbiology, clinical pathology, haematology, blood banking, histopathology and cytopathology.
bullets: Specialty-specific analytical testing and specimen/sample handling.
Quality-system discipline: controls, proficiency testing, traceable records and corrective follow-up.
Equipment upkeep, periodic calibration and basic troubleshooting within authorised procedures.
Accurate digital documentation, including LIMS or programme data systems.
Clear communication and practical support for staff training and assessment activities.

sections.heading: Workplaces and work context
paragraphs: Senior lab technicians may work in hospitals, medical diagnostic laboratories, public-health programme laboratories, research institutes and university or technical laboratories. Under the Clinical Establishments framework, laboratories and diagnostic centres carrying out pathological, bacteriological, genetic, radiological, chemical or biological investigations are clinical establishments. The ICMR example shows a public-health/research laboratory context, while Lady Hardinge Medical College illustrates a hospital diagnostic-centre project context.

The work is normally site-based because it involves specimens, testing platforms, laboratory equipment, quality records and, in some posts, patient-facing collection or phlebotomy. The National Career Service describes laboratory technician/assistant work as typically not work-from-home and notes pharmaceutical and biopharmaceutical units among probable employers; a senior role may build on this environment with greater technical and quality responsibility.
bullets: Hospital pathology, microbiology, genetics and blood-related laboratories.
Government disease-control, surveillance and external-quality-assurance programmes.
Medical-college, research-institute and diagnostic-centre laboratories.
Pharmaceutical, biopharmaceutical and other life-science laboratory operations.

sections.heading: Progression
paragraphs: A common experience-led path is Helper → Junior Lab Technician → Lab Technician/Assistant → Senior Lab Technician → Lab Manager, according to the National Career Service. This should be read as a career illustration rather than a guaranteed hierarchy: employers may use titles such as Senior Medical Lab Technician, Technical Assistant or Technologist instead.

Some organisations have formal internal advancement systems. ISRO’s career information for Lab Technician-A says that an employee can progress up to Senior Lab Technician-B in Pay Level 10 through its Merit Promotion Scheme, subject to satisfactory performance. This is an organisation-specific route, but it demonstrates that progression may be through merit promotion rather than external recruitment alone.
bullets: Deepen experience in a high-demand specialty such as microbiology, molecular diagnostics, genetics, blood banking or quality assurance.
Take responsibility for calibration, quality records, data review and staff capability-building.
Progress toward laboratory supervision, laboratory management, specialist technical roles or institution-specific senior technical grades, where available.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: Pay is highly variable by employer type, location, specialty, contract status and pay framework; it should be checked in the current vacancy notice rather than inferred from a job title. As dated examples, an ICMR project advertisement listed a consolidated ₹25,000 per month for a temporary Project Senior Lab Technician post, while ISRO’s career information states that Lab Technician-A can progress to Senior Lab Technician-B at Pay Level 10, with a displayed pay-matrix range of ₹56,100–₹1,77,500. These are not market-wide salary benchmarks and do not include a like-for-like comparison of allowances, tenure or selection conditions.

For context on entry-level progression rather than senior pay, the National Career Service’s Lab Technician/Assistant page labels its experience-based earnings figure as indicative and subject to change. Candidates should compare the basic pay or consolidated amount, allowances, pension/benefit eligibility, contract duration, shift obligations and location before applying.
bullets: Project appointments may offer a fixed consolidated monthly amount and limited tenure.
Regular public-sector roles may use a pay-matrix level with allowances and promotion rules set by that employer.
Private-sector and specialised laboratory pay should be verified directly with the employer because published ranges vary substantially.
